 Replaces "ReplaceChr" in "s" with "Withstr" in "d" won't copy more than "l" bytes to "d" Replaces all occurrences of a character in a string with another string, copying the result to a destination buffer. It ensures that the total length of the resulting string does not exceed the specified limit, and that the result is null-terminated. This function is useful for simple string substitutions where you want to replace a single character with a longer string, such as replacing spaces with underscores or tabs with spaces.

Detailed Description

Headder for C-Menu API library, libcm.so.

Author
Bill Waller Copyright (c) 2025 MIT License billxwaller@gmail.com
Date
2026-02-09

Definition in file cm.h.

Macro Definition Documentation

◆ __atexit

#define __atexit
Value:
{ \
int rc; \
rc = atexit(end_pgm); \
if (rc != 0) { \
fprintf(stderr, "\nCannot set exit function\n"); \
exit(EXIT_FAILURE); \
} \
}

This macro registers the end_pgm function to be called when the program exits.

Note
It checks the return value of atexit() to ensure that the registration was successful, and if not, it prints an error message and exits with a failure status. Programs using libcm should call __atexit in their main function to ensure that the end_pgm function is registered to be called on program exit. This will help ensure that the terminal is properly restored to its original state, even if the program encounters an error or is terminated unexpectedly.

85 exit(EXIT_FAILURE); \
86 } \
87 }

Referenced by main().

◆ __end_pgm

#define __end_pgm
Value:
static void end_pgm(void) { \
win_del(); \
destroy_curses(); \
restore_shell_tioctl(); \
sig_dfl_mode(); \
exit(EXIT_FAILURE); \
}

This macro defines the function, end_pgm, that is responsible for cleaning up the terminal state and exiting the program.

Note
This function is typically registered to be called when the program exits, ensuring that the terminal is properly restored to its original state, even if the program encounters an error or is terminated unexpectedly. The end_pgm function performs the following actions:
  1. It calls win_del() to delete any windows that may have been created during the program's execution.
  2. It calls destroy_curses() to clean up the ncurses library and restore the terminal to its normal state.
  3. It calls restore_shell_tioctl() to restore the terminal's input/output settings to their original state.
  4. Finally, it calls exit(EXIT_FAILURE) to terminate the program with a failure status, indicating that the program has encountered an error or is exiting due to an unexpected condition. By defining this function and registering it to be called on program exit, you can help ensure that the terminal is properly cleaned up and restored, preventing any issues with the terminal state after the program has exited.

63 static void end_pgm(void) { \
64 win_del(); \
65 destroy_curses(); \

67 sig_dfl_mode(); \
68 exit(EXIT_FAILURE); \
69 }

◆ KEY_ALTINS

#define KEY_ALTINS   0x223

EXTENDED NCURSES KEYS

Key code bindings are customarily defined in terminfo. xterm-256color tends to be the most complete, and indeed, it seems to work with Ghostty, Kitty, and Alacritty. If you want to use the altkey bindings, you may need to set your terminal's TERM environment variable to xterm-256color. Here's why:

Ghostty, Kitty, and Alacritty all come with their own terminfo files:

Ghostty with xterm-ghostty, Kitty with xterm-kitty, and Alacritty with alacritty

None of the three produced the correct extended altkey bindings when paired with the terminfo provided in their distribution, yet all three worked with xterm-256color.

The extended altkeys were not defined in any of the terminfo files, not even xterm-256color. Yet, xterm-256color produced consistent keycode bindings with all three emulators. The standard key bindings, ins, delete, up, down arrows, etc., were the same for all three terminfos, and all three emulators produced the correct key bindings for those keys.

The fact that xterm-256color produced consistent keycode bindings for the altkeys, while the other three terminfos did not, is likely due to the fact that xterm-256color is more widely used and has better support for extended key bindings, even if they are not explicitly defined in the terminfo file. It's possible that xterm-256color has some default behavior or fallback mechanism that allows it to recognize and produce key codes for the altkeys, while the other terminfos do not have such a mechanism in place. This could explain why xterm-256color produced consistent keycode bindings for the altkeys across all three emulators, while the other terminfos did not.

Referenced by display_curses_keys().

◆ apply_gamma()

void apply_gamma ( RGB * rgb)
extern

Apply gamma correction to RGB color.

Parameters
rgbPointer to RGB color
Note
This function modifies the RGB color in place. It applies gamma correction to the RGB color based on the gamma values set in the SIO struct. If the color is a shade of gray, it applies the gray gamma correction. Otherwise, it applies the individual red, green, and blue gamma corrections.

Definition at line 507 of file dwin.c.

507 {
508 if (rgb->r == rgb->g && rgb->r == rgb->b) {
509 if (GRAY_GAMMA > 0.0f && GRAY_GAMMA != 1.0f) {
510 rgb->r = (int)(pow((rgb->r / 255.0f), 1.0f / GRAY_GAMMA) * 255.0f);
511 rgb->g = rgb->r;
512 rgb->b = rgb->r;
513 }
514 return;
515 }
516 if (rgb->r != 0 && RED_GAMMA > 0.0f && RED_GAMMA != 1.0f)
517 rgb->r = (int)(pow((rgb->r / 255.0f), 1.0f / RED_GAMMA) * 255.0f);
518 if (rgb->g != 0 && GREEN_GAMMA > 0.0f && GREEN_GAMMA != 1.0f)
519 rgb->g = (int)(pow((rgb->g / 255.0f), 1.0f / GREEN_GAMMA) * 255.0f);
520 if (rgb->b != 0 && BLUE_GAMMA > 0.0f && BLUE_GAMMA != 1.0f)
521 rgb->b = (int)(pow((rgb->b / 255.0f), 1.0f / BLUE_GAMMA) * 255.0f);
522}

◆ cbox()

void cbox ( WINDOW * box)
extern

Draw a box around the specified window.

Parameters
boxPointer to the window to draw the box around
Note
This function uses NCurses functions to draw a box around the specified window. It adds the appropriate characters for the corners and edges of the box based on the current character set. Use this function when you want to visually separate a window from the rest of the screen with a border.

◆ compile_chyron()

void compile_chyron ( Chyron * chyron)
extern

construct the chyron string from the chyron structure

Parameters
chyron

The chyron string is constructed by concatenating the labels of the set keys, separated by " | ". The end_pos values for each key are set to determine the zones for mouse clicks. When a mouse click occurs, the get_chyron_key function uses the end_pos values to determine which key was clicked based on the X position of the click.

Definition at line 252 of file dwin.c.

253 int end_pos = 0;
254 int i = 0;
255 chyron->s[0] = '\0';
256 chyron->l = 0;
257 while (i < CHYRON_KEYS) {
258 if (chyron->key[i]->text[0] == '\0') {
259 i++;
260 continue;
261 }
262 if (end_pos == 0)
263 strnz__cat(chyron->s, " ", MAXLEN - 1);
264 else
265 strnz__cat(chyron->s, " | ", MAXLEN - 1);
266 strnz__cat(chyron->s, chyron->key[i]->text, MAXLEN - 1);
267 end_pos = strlen(chyron->s) + 1;
268 chyron->key[i]->end_pos = end_pos;
269 i++;
270 }
271 if (end_pos > 0)
272 chyron->l = strnz__cat(chyron->s, " ", MAXLEN - 1);
273}

◆ destroy_curses()

void destroy_curses ( )
extern

Gracefully shut down NCurses and restore terminal settings.

Note
This function should be called before exiting the program to ensure that the terminal is left in a usable state. It checks if NCurses was initialized and, if so, it clears the screen, refreshes it, and ends the NCurses session. It also restores the original terminal settings using restore_shell_tioctl and resets signal handlers to their default state with sig_dfl_mode.

Definition at line 609 of file dwin.c.

609 {
610 if (f_curses_open) {
611 wclear(stdscr);
612 wrefresh(stdscr);
613 endwin();
614 f_curses_open = false;
615 }
616 restore_shell_tioctl();
617 sig_dfl_mode();
618 return;
619}
f_curses_open
bool f_curses_open
Definition sig.c:20

References f_curses_open, restore_shell_tioctl(), and sig_dfl_mode().

452 case KEY_MOUSE:
453 action[0] = '\0';
454 if (getmouse(&event) == OK) {
455 switch (event.bstate) {
456 case BUTTON1_PRESSED:
457 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 1 pressed", KSTRLEN - 1);
458 break;
459 case BUTTON1_RELEASED:
460 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 1 released", KSTRLEN - 1);
461 break;
462 case BUTTON1_CLICKED:
463 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 1 clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
464 break;
465 case BUTTON1_DOUBLE_CLICKED:
466 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 1 double-clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
467 break;
468 case BUTTON2_PRESSED:
469 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 2 pressed", KSTRLEN - 1);
470 break;
471 case BUTTON2_RELEASED:
472 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 2 released", KSTRLEN - 1);
473 break;
474 case BUTTON2_CLICKED:
475 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 2 clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
476 break;
477 case BUTTON2_DOUBLE_CLICKED:
478 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 2 double-clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
479 break;
480 case BUTTON3_PRESSED:
481 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 3 pressed", KSTRLEN - 1);
482 break;
483 case BUTTON3_RELEASED:
484 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 3 released", KSTRLEN - 1);
485 break;
486 case BUTTON3_CLICKED:
487 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 3 clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
488 break;
489 case BUTTON3_DOUBLE_CLICKED:
490 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 3 double-clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
491 break;
492 case BUTTON4_PRESSED:
493 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 4 pressed", KSTRLEN - 1);
494 break;
495 case BUTTON4_RELEASED:
496 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 4 released", KSTRLEN - 1);
497 break;
498 case BUTTON4_CLICKED:
499 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 4 clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
500 break;
501 case BUTTON4_DOUBLE_CLICKED:
502 strnz__cpy(action, "Button 4 double-clicked", KSTRLEN - 1);
503 break;
504 case BUTTON5_PRESSED:
505 strnz__cpy(action, "Scroll Up", KSTRLEN - 1);
506 break;
507 default:
508 break;
509 }
510 mvwaddstr(win, 6, 3, " Action:");
511 mvwaddstr(win, 6, 16, action);
512 wclrtoeol(win);
513
514 strnz__cpy(tmp, " With Key:", MAXLEN - 1);
515 if (event.bstate & BUTTON_SHIFT)
516 strnz__cat(tmp, " Shift", MAXLEN - 1);
517 if (event.bstate & BUTTON_CTRL)
518 strnz__cat(tmp, " Ctrl", MAXLEN - 1);
519 if (event.bstate & BUTTON_ALT)
520 strnz__cat(tmp, " Alt", MAXLEN - 1);
521 mvwaddstr(win, 3, 4, tmp);
522 wclrtoeol(win);
523 if (wenclose(win, event.y, event.x)) {
524 sprintf(tmp, " Inside Win: y: %3d, x: %3d",
525 event.y - begy, event.x - begx);
526 } else {
527 sprintf(tmp, " Outside Win: y: %3d, x: %3d",
528 event.y - begy, event.x - begx);
529 }
530 mvwaddstr(win, 4, 4, tmp);
531 wclrtoeol(win);
532 wmove(win, 5, 0);
533 wclrtoeol(win);
534 wmove(win, 7, 0);
535 wclrtoeol(win);
536 }
537 break;
538 default:
539 break;
540 }
541 if (c != KEY_MOUSE) {
542
543 sprintf(tmp, " Octal: %3o", c);
544 mvwaddstr(win, 3, 4, tmp);
545 wclrtoeol(win);
546
547 sprintf(tmp, " Decimal: %3d", c);
548 mvwaddstr(win, 4, 4, tmp);
549 wclrtoeol(win);
550
551 sprintf(tmp, " Hex: %3x", c);
552 mvwaddstr(win, 5, 4, tmp);
553 wclrtoeol(win);
554
555 if (kstr[0]) {
556 sprintf(tmp, "Description: %s", kstr);
557 } else {
558 sprintf(tmp, " ASCII: %c", c);
559 }
560 mvwaddstr(win, 6, 3, tmp);
561 wclrtoeol(win);
562 mvwaddstr(win, 7, 2, "Key bound To: ");
563 wclrtoeol(win);
564 mvwaddstr(win, 7, 16, s ? s : "Not Bound");
565 wrefresh(win);
566 }
567 if (c == KEY_ALTEND) {
568 usleep(100000);
569 break;
570 }
571 c = '\0';
572 }
573 wclear(win);
574 wrefresh(win);
575 clear();
576 refresh();
577 win_del();
578 return 0;
579}

char * em1,

857 Chyron *chyron = new_chyron();
858 set_chyron_key(chyron, 1, "F1 Help", KEY_F(1));
859 set_chyron_key(chyron, 9, "F9 Cancel", KEY_F(9));
860 set_chyron_key(chyron, 10, "F10 Continue", KEY_F(10));
861 compile_chyron(chyron);
862
863 em_l = 0;
864 em0_l = strnz(em0, COLS - 4);

871 em_l = em1_l;

873 em_l = em2_l;

877 em_l = chyron->l;

879 em_l = COLS - 4;
880
881 pos = ((COLS - em_l) - 4) / 2;
882 line = (LINES - 6) / 2;
883 strnz__cpy(title, "Notification", MAXLEN - 1);
884 if (win_new(5, em_l + 2, line, pos, title, 0)) {
885 ssnprintf(title, MAXLEN - 1, "win_new(%d, %d, %d, %d, %s, %b) failed",
886 5, em_l + 2, line, pos, title, 0);
887 destroy_chyron(chyron);
888 abend(-1, title);
889 }

897 wattroff(error_win, WA_REVERSE);
898 wmove(error_win, 4, chyron->l + 1);

903 case 'n':

907 case 'x':

911 case KEY_F(1):

914 win_del();
915 return (cmd_key);

920 win_del();
921 destroy_chyron(chyron);
922 return (cmd_key);

bool expand_tilde ( char * path,
int path_maxlen )
extern

Replace Leading Tilde With Home Directory.

Parameters
path- path to expand
path_maxlen- maximum length of path
Note
The caller is responsible for ensuring that "path" has enough space to receive the result, and that "path_maxlen" is sufficient to hold the result. This function does not perform any bounds checking on "path", so it is the caller's responsibility to ensure that it is valid and that "path_maxlen" is appropriate for the operation.
Returns
true if successful

◆ fork_exec()

int fork_exec ( char ** argv)
extern

Fork and exec a command.

Parameters
argv- array of arguments for the command to execute
Returns
the return code from the executed command, or -1 on error
Note
Captures and restores terminal settings around the fork and exec.
Sets signal handlers to default in the child process.
Waits for the child process to complete in the parent process.
Handles errors from fork and execvp, and reports child exit status.
Restores curses mode and keypad settings after execution.
Restores window states after execution.
Uses a temporary string buffer tmp_str for error messages.
Uses Perror for error reporting.
Uses sig_dfl_mode and sig_prog_mode for signal handling.
Uses capture_curses_tioctl and restore_curses_tioctl for terminal settings.
Uses restore_shell_tioctl for shell terminal settings.
Uses waitpid to wait for the child process.
Uses WIFEXITED, WEXITSTATUS, WIFSIGNALED, and WTERMSIG to interpret child status.
Uses keypad to manage keypad mode in curses.
Uses restore_wins to restore window states.
Uses errno for error codes.
Uses pid_t for process IDs.
Uses standard file descriptors STDIN_FILENO, STDOUT_FILENO, STDERR_FILENO.
Uses execvp for executing the command.
Uses fork for creating a new process.
Uses ssnprintf for formatting error messages.
Uses switch-case for handling fork results.
Uses default shell if SHELL environment variable is not set.

Definition at line 124 of file exec.c.

124 {
125 char tmp_str[MAXLEN];
126 pid_t pid;
127 int status;
128 int rc;
129
130 if (argv[0] == 0) {
131 Perror("fork_exec: missing argument for execvp");
132 return (-1);
133 }
134 capture_curses_tioctl();
135 sig_dfl_mode();
136 restore_shell_tioctl();
137
138 tmp_str[0] = '\0';
139 pid = fork();
140 switch (pid) {
141 case -1: // parent fork failed
142 restore_curses_tioctl();
143 sig_prog_mode();
144 keypad(stdscr, true);
145 ssnprintf(tmp_str, sizeof(tmp_str), "fork failed: %s, errno: %d",
146 argv[0], errno);
147 Perror(tmp_str);
148 return (-1);
149 case 0: // child
150 restore_shell_tioctl();
151 execvp(argv[0], argv);
152 restore_curses_tioctl();
153 sig_prog_mode();
154 keypad(stdscr, true);
155 ssnprintf(tmp_str, sizeof(tmp_str), "execvp failed: %s, errno: %d",
156 argv[0], errno);
157 Perror(tmp_str);
158 exit(-1);
159 default: // parent
160 rc = 0;
161 restore_curses_tioctl();
162 sig_prog_mode();
163 waitpid(pid, &status, 0);
164 if (WIFEXITED(status)) {
165 rc = WEXITSTATUS(status);
166 if (rc != 0) {
167 keypad(stdscr, true);
168 ssnprintf(tmp_str, sizeof(tmp_str),
169 "Child %s exited with status %d", argv[0], rc);
170 }
171 } else {
172 if (WIFSIGNALED(status)) {
173 rc = WTERMSIG(status);
174 keypad(stdscr, true);
175 ssnprintf(tmp_str, sizeof(tmp_str),
176 "Child %s terminated by signal %d", argv[0], rc);
177 } else {
178 keypad(stdscr, true);
179 ssnprintf(tmp_str, sizeof(tmp_str),
180 "Child %s terminated abnormally", argv[0]);
181 }
182 }
183 break;
184 }
185 restore_curses_tioctl();
186 sig_prog_mode();
187 keypad(stdscr, true);
188 restore_wins();
189 if (tmp_str[0] != '\0') {
190 Perror(tmp_str);
191 }
192 return (rc);
193}
restore_curses_tioctl
bool restore_curses_tioctl()
restore_curses_tioctl() - restore curses terminal settings

◆ get_chyron_key()

int get_chyron_key ( Chyron * chyron,
int x )
extern

Get keycode from chyron.

Parameters
chyronstructure
xMouse X position
Returns
Keycode
Note
This function uses the end_pos values set in compile_chyron to determine which key was clicked
The chyron functions provide xwgetch() with a mechanism to translate mouse click positions into key codes based on the labels set in the chyron structure. When a mouse click occurs, xwgetch() can call get_chyron_key() with the X position of the click to determine which function key was clicked, allowing for dynamic and customizable function key behavior in the chyron area of the interface.

291 break;
292 return chyron->key[i]->keycode;
293}

◆ init_clr_palette()

bool init_clr_palette ( SIO * sio)
extern

Initialize color palette based on SIO settings.

Parameters
sioPointer to SIO struct with color settings
Returns
true if successful, false if error
Note
This function initializes the xterm256 color cube and applies any color overrides specified in the SIO struct. The color strings in the SIO struct are expected to be six-digit HTML style hex color codes (e.g., "#RRGGBB"). If a color override is specified for any of the standard colors, it is applied using the init_hex_clr function. After processing all colors, the clr_cnt variable is set to 16 to indicate that the standard colors have been initialized.

539 if (sio->green[0])
540 init_hex_clr(CLR_GREEN, sio->green);

◆ lf_find()

bool lf_find ( const char * base_path,
const char * re,
const char * ere,
int max_depth,
int flags )
extern

Find files in a directory matching a regular expression.

Parameters
base_pathdirectory to search
ereregular expression match to exclude
reregular expression match to include
max_depthdepth of directories to scan
flagssearch flags
flags = LF_ALL | LF_ICASE | LF_FILES
flags = LF_ALL | LF_ICASE | LF_DIRS
LF_ALL = 1, List all files including hidden files
LF_ICASE = 2, Ignore case in search
LF_FILES = 4, list files (exclude directories)
LF_DIRS = 8, list directories (exclude files)
LF_EXCLUDE = 16, exclude files matching "ere" pattern
LF_REGEX = 32, re is a regular expression pattern to match
search
bool search(View *, int *, char *)
Search for Regular Expression Pattern.

return true if successful, false otherwise

909 regex_t compiled_re;
910 regex_t compiled_ere;
911 int REG_FLAGS = REG_EXTENDED;
912
913 if (flags & LF_ICASE)
914 REG_FLAGS |= REG_ICASE;
915 if (flags & LF_REGEX) {
916 reti = regcomp(&compiled_re, re, REG_FLAGS);
917 if (reti) {
918 ssnprintf(em0, MAXLEN - 1, "lf: \'%s\' Invalid pattern\n", re);
919 ssnprintf(em1, MAXLEN - 1, "for example: \'.*\\.c$\'\n\n");
920 regerror(reti, &compiled_re, em2, sizeof(em2));
921 display_error(em0, em1, em2, nullptr);
922 regfree(&compiled_re);
923 return false;
924 }
925 }
926 if (flags & LF_EXCLUDE) {
927 regcomp(&compiled_ere, "^$", REG_FLAGS);

◆ mk_raw_tioctl()

bool mk_raw_tioctl ( struct termios * t_p)
extern

mk_raw_tioctl() - set terminal to raw mode

Parameters
t_p- pointer to termios structure to modify
Returns
- true on success
Note
- unlike cfmakeraw(), this leaves ISIG enabled.
- cfmakeraw() disables ISIG, which prevents C-MENU from handling signals like Ctrl-C. Instead of using cfmakeraw(), we manually set the terminal to raw mode while leaving ISIG enabled.
- the following code is equivalent to cfmakeraw() but with ISIG left enabled:
t_p->c_iflag &= ~(IGNBRK | BRKINT | PARMRK | INPCK | ISTRIP | INLCR |
IGNCR | ICRNL | IXON | IXOFF);
t_p->c_lflag &= ~(ECHO | ECHONL | ICANON | IEXTEN);
t_p->c_cflag &= ~(CSIZE | PARENB);
t_p->c_cflag |= CS8 | CLOCAL;
Examples
/usr/local/src/cmenu/src/enterchr.c.

Definition at line 110 of file scriou.c.

110 {
111 tcgetattr(0, t_p);
112 t_p->c_lflag |= ISIG;
113 t_p->c_lflag &= ~(ECHO | ICANON);
114 t_p->c_cc[VMIN] = 1;
115 t_p->c_cc[VTIME] = 0;
116 tcsetattr(0, TCSAFLUSH, t_p);
117 return true;
118}

Here is the caller graph for this function:

◆ new_chyron()

Chyron * new_chyron ( )
extern

Create and initialize Chyron structure.

Returns
pointer to new Chyron structure

This function allocates memory for a new Chyron structure and initializes the key pointers. Each key pointer is allocated memory for a ChyronKey structure. The Chyron structure is used to manage function key labels and their associated keycodes for mouse click handling in the chyron area of the interface.

Note
The use of calloc ensures that the allocated memory is initialized to zero, which means that the text for each key will be initialized to an empty string and the keycodes will be initialized to zero. This allows the is_set_chyron_key function to check if a key is set by checking if the first character of the text is not '\0'. If any memory allocation fails, the function will call abend to handle the error and return nullptr.

Definition at line 178 of file dwin.c.

178 {
179 Chyron *chyron = (Chyron *)calloc(1, sizeof(Chyron));
180 if (!chyron) {
181 abend(-1, "calloc chyron failed");
182 return nullptr;
183 }
184 for (int i = 0; i < CHYRON_KEYS; i++)
185 chyron->key[i] = (ChyronKey *)calloc(1, sizeof(ChyronKey));
186 return chyron;
187}
ChyronKey
Definition cm.h:116

References abend(), CHYRON_KEYS, and Chyron::key.

◆ open_curses()

bool open_curses ( SIO * sio)
extern

Initialize NCurses and color settings.

Parameters
sioPointer to SIO struct with terminal and color settings
Returns
true if successful, false if error note This function initializes NCurses and sets up color pairs based on the settings in the SIO struct. It also applies gamma correction to colors. Use this function to initialize NCurses if you don't want NCurses to receive data from the stdin pipe
1. saves stdin and stdout file descriptors in SIO
2. opens a terminal device for NCurses screen IO
3. replaces STDERR_FILENO with terminal file descriptor
SIO
The SIO structure encapsulates various aspects of the terminal's state and configuration,...

◆ rep_substring()

char * rep_substring ( const char * org_s,
const char * tgt_s,
const char * rep_s )
extern

Replace all occurrences of "tgt_s" in "org_s" with "rep_s".

Parameters
org_s- original string
tgt_s- target substring to replace
rep_s- replacement substring
Returns
A pointer to the newly allocated string with replacements or a copy of the replacement string if original string is the same as target string This is a special case that allows for replacing the entire original string. If any parameter is nullptr, the function returns nullptr. If "tgt_s" is not found in "org_s", the function returns a copy of "org_s". If target substring is not found the function returns a copy of the original string.
Note
The function allocates memory for the return value, so the caller is responsible for freeing this memory when it is no longer needed to avoid memory leaks.
The function does not modify the original string "org_s".
The function assumes that "tgt_s" and "rep_s" are null-terminated strings. If they are not, the behavior is undefined.
The function does not perform any bounds checking on the input strings, so it is the caller's responsibility to ensure that they are valid and that the resulting string does not exceed available memory.
The function uses the standard library functions strlen, strstr, malloc, and strcpy, which may have their own limitations and behaviors that the caller should be aware of.
The function does not handle overlapping occurrences of "tgt_s" in "org_s". If "tgt_s" can overlap with itself in "org_s", the behavior may be unexpected. The caller should ensure that "tgt_s" does not contain overlapping patterns to avoid this issue.
The function does not handle cases where "tgt_s" is a substring of "rep_s", which could lead to unintended consequences if "tgt_s" appears in "rep_s". The caller should ensure that "tgt_s" and "rep_s" are distinct to avoid this issue.

Definition at line 1138 of file futil.c.

◆ restore_wins()

void restore_wins ( )
extern

Restore all windows after a screen resize.

Note
This function is used to restore the display of all windows after a screen resize event. It clears the standard screen and then iterates through all existing windows, touching and refreshing them to ensure they are redrawn correctly on the resized screen. Use this function in response to a SIGWINCH signal to handle terminal resizing gracefully.

◆ rgb_to_xterm256_idx()

int rgb_to_xterm256_idx ( RGB * rgb)
extern

Convert RGB color to XTerm 256 color index.

Parameters
rgbRGB color
Returns
XTerm 256 color index note This function converts an RGB color to the nearest XTerm 256 color index. It first checks if the color is a shade of gray, and if so, it uses the gray ramp. Otherwise, it calculates the nearest color in the 6x6x6 color cube.

454 if (rgb->r == rgb->g && rgb->g == rgb->b) {
455 if (rgb->r < 8)
456 return 16;
457 if (rgb->r > 248)
458 return 231;
459 return ((rgb->r - 8) / 10) + 231;
460 } else {
461 int r_index = (rgb->r < 45) ? 0 : (rgb->r - 60) / 40 + 1;
462 int g_index = (rgb->g < 45) ? 0 : (rgb->g - 60) / 40 + 1;
463 int b_index = (rgb->b < 45) ? 0 : (rgb->b - 60) / 40 + 1;
464 return 16 + (36 * r_index) + (6 * g_index) + b_index;
465 }
466}

References RGB::b, RGB::g, and RGB::r.

◆ set_chyron_key()

void set_chyron_key ( Chyron * chyron,
int k,
char * s,
int kc )
extern

Set chyron key.

Parameters
chyronstructure
kchyron key index (0-19)
schyron key label
kcchyron key code

This function sets the label and keycode for a chyron key. The label is copied into the chyron structure, and the keycode is stored for later retrieval when theire is a mouse click. The compile_chyron function uses the keycode values to determine which key was clicked based on the mouse X position. If the label string is empty, the key is unset by setting the first character of the text to '\0'.

◆ sig_prog_mode()

void sig_prog_mode ( )
extern

Set up signal handlers for interrupt signals.

Upon receiving an interrupt signal (SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGQUIT), the program will prompt the user to either exit the program or continue execution. If the user chooses to exit, a confirmation prompt will be displayed. If the user opts to continue, the program will resume normal operation.

Examples
/usr/local/src/cmenu/src/enterstr.c.

Definition at line 42 of file sig.c.

42 {
43 struct sigaction sa;
44
45 memset(&sa, 0, sizeof(sa));
46 sa.sa_handler = signal_handler;
47 sigemptyset(&sa.sa_mask);
48 sa.sa_flags = SA_RESTART;
49 if (sigaction(SIGINT, &sa, nullptr) == -1) {
50 abend(-1, "sigaction SIGINT failed");
51 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
52 }
53 if (sigaction(SIGTERM, &sa, nullptr) == -1) {
54 abend(-1, "sigaction SIGTERM failed");
55 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
56 };
57 if (sigaction(SIGQUIT, &sa, nullptr) == -1) {
58 abend(-1, "sigaction SIGQUIT failed");
59 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
60 }
61 if (sigaction(SIGUSR1, &sa, nullptr) == -1) {
62 abend(-1, "sigaction SIGUSR1 failed");
63 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
64 }
65 sa.sa_flags = SA_SIGINFO; // Set the flag to receive siginfo_t
66 if (sigaction(SIGSEGV, &sa, nullptr) == -1) {
67 abend(-1, "sigaction SIGSEGV failed");
68 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
69 }
70}

◆ ssnprintf()

size_t ssnprintf ( char * buf,
size_t buf_size,
const char * format,
... )
extern

ssnprintf was designed to be a safer alternative to snprintf.

It ensures that the buffer is not overflowed by taking the buffer size as a parameter and using vsnprintf internally. It also returns the number of characters that would have been written if enough space had been available, allowing the caller to detect truncation. This function is particularly useful in situations where the formatted string may exceed the buffer size, as it prevents buffer overflows and provides a way to handle such cases gracefully.

Parameters
buf- buffer to receive formatted string
buf_size- size of buffer
format- printf-style format string
...- arguments
Returns
number of characters that would have been written if enough space had been available

Definition at line 111 of file futil.c.

111 {
112 size_t n;
113 va_list args;
114
115 va_start(args, format);
116 n = vsnprintf(buf, buf_size, format, args);
117 va_end(args);
118
119 return n;
120}

Referenced by display_error(), enter_file_spec(), fork_exec(), form_calculate(), form_desc_error(), form_parse_desc(), form_write(), get_clr_pair(), handle_signal(), init_view_boxwin(), init_view_full_screen(), lf_find(), lf_process(), mapp_initialization(), menu_engine(), mk_dir(), new_init(), new_pick(), new_view(), open_pick_win(), parse_menu_description(), Perror(), pick_display_chyron(), search(), set_chyron_key(), verify_dir(), verify_file(), view_cmd_processor(), view_init_input(), and write_view_buffer().

◆ str_subc()

bool str_subc ( char * d,
char * s,
char ReplaceChr,
char * Withstr,
int l )
extern

Replaces "ReplaceChr" in "s" with "Withstr" in "d" won't copy more than "l" bytes to "d" Replaces all occurrences of a character in a string with another string, copying the result to a destination buffer. It ensures that the total length of the resulting string does not exceed the specified limit, and that the result is null-terminated. This function is useful for simple string substitutions where you want to replace a single character with a longer string, such as replacing spaces with underscores or tabs with spaces.

Parameters
d- destination string
s- source string
ReplaceChr- character to replace
Withstr- string to insert
l- maximum length to copy
Returns
true if successful, false if any parameter is invalid
Note
The caller must ensure that "d" has enough space to receive the result, and that "l" is sufficient to hold the result. This function does not perform any bounds checking on "d" or "Withstr", so it is the caller's responsibility to ensure that they are valid and that "l" is appropriate for the operation.

Definition at line 353 of file futil.c.

353 {
354 char *e;
355 if (s == nullptr || d == nullptr || Withstr == nullptr || l == 0) {

357 *d = '\0';
358 return false;
359 }
360 e = d + l;
361 while (*s != '\0' && d < e) {
362 if (*s == ReplaceChr) {
363 while (*Withstr != '\0' && d < e)
364 *d++ = *Withstr++;
365 s++;
366 } else
367 *d++ = *s++;
368 }
369 *d = '\0';
370 return true;
371}

Converts a string into an array of argument strings.

Note
Handles quoted strings and escaped quotes, preserving text inside quotes as individual arguments. It has been in service for many years without problems.
Parameters
argv- array of pointers to arguments
arg_str- string containing arguments
max_args- maximum number of arguments to parse
Returns
argc, a count of allocated vectors in argv
Note
the caller is responsible for deallocating the strings in argv

137 char *d = tmp_str;
138
139 while (*p != '\0' && argc < max_args) {
140 while (isspace((unsigned char)*p))
141 p++;
142 if (*p == '\0')
143 break;
144 if (*p == '"') {
145 in_quotes = 1;
146 p++;
147 }
148 while (*p != '\0') {
149 if (in_quotes) {
150 if (*p == '\\' && *(p + 1) == '"') {
151 *d++ = '"';
152 p += 2;
153 } else if (*p == '"') {
154 *d++ = '\0';
155 p++;

157 break;
158 } else

160 } else {

162 *d++ = '\0';
163 p++;
164 break;
165 } else
166 *d++ = *p++;
167 }
168 }
169 *d = '\0';
170 d = tmp_str;
171 argv[argc++] = strdup(tmp_str);

173 argv[argc] = nullptr;
174 return argc;

Here is the caller graph for this function:

◆ strnz__cat()

size_t strnz__cat ( char * d,
const char * s,
size_t max_len )
extern

safer alternative to strncat

Note
It appends string s to d, ensuring that the total length of d does not exceed max_len, and that the resulting string is null-terminated. It also treats newline and carriage return characters as string terminators, preventing them from being included in the result. This is particularly useful when concatenating user input or file data, where embedded newlines could cause issues.
Parameters
d- destination string
s- source string
max_len- maximum length to copy
Returns
length of resulting string

Definition at line 241 of file futil.c.

241 {
242 char *e;
243 size_t len = 0;
244 if (s == nullptr || d == nullptr || max_len == 0) {
245 if (d != nullptr && max_len > 0)
246 *d = '\0';
247 return 0;
248 }
249 e = d + max_len;
250 while (*d != '\0' && *d != '\n' && *d != '\r' && d < e) {
251 d++;
252 len++;
253 }
254 while (*s != '\0' && *s != '\n' && *s != '\r' && d < e) {
255 *d++ = *s++;
256 len++;
257 }
258 *d = '\0';
259 return len;
260}

◆ strnz__cpy()

size_t strnz__cpy ( char * d,
const char * s,
size_t max_len )
extern

safer alternative to strncpy

Note
It copies string s to d, ensuring that the total length of d does not exceed max_len, and that the resulting string is null-terminated. It also treats newline and carriage return characters as string terminators, preventing them from being included in the result. This is particularly useful when copying user input or file data, where embedded newlines could cause issues.
Parameters
d- destination string
s- source string
max_len- maximum length to copy
Returns
length of resulting string

◆ win_del()

WINDOW * win_del ( )
extern

Delete the current window and its associated box window.

Returns
nullptr
Note
This function deletes the current window and its associated box window, if they exist. It also refreshes the remaining windows to ensure the display is updated correctly. After calling this function, the global win_ptr variable is decremented to point to the previous window in the stack.

◆ win_redraw()

void win_redraw ( WINDOW * win)
extern

Redraw the specified window.

Parameters
winPointer to the window to redraw
Note
This function erases the contents of the specified window and then refreshes it to update the display. Use this function when you need to clear and redraw a window, such as after resizing or when updating its contents.

◆ win_resize()

void win_resize ( int wlines,
int wcols,
char * title )
extern

Resize the current window and its box, and update the title.

Parameters
wlinesNumber of lines
wcolsNumber of columns
titleWindow title
Note
This function resizes the current window and its associated box window to the specified number of lines and columns. It also updates the title of the box window if a title is provided. After resizing, it refreshes the windows to apply the changes.

◆ xterm256_idx_to_rgb()

RGB xterm256_idx_to_rgb ( int idx)
extern

Convert XTerm 256 color index to RGB color.

Parameters
idxXTerm 256 color index
Returns
RGB color note This function converts an XTerm 256 color index to an RGB color. It first checks if the index is in the standard 16 colors, then checks if it's in the 6x6x6 color cube, and finally checks if it's in the gray ramp.

Convert XTerm 256 color index to RGB

Parameters
idx- XTerm 256 color index
Returns
RGB struct

Definition at line 474 of file dwin.c.

474 {
478 RGB rgb;
479 if (idx > 255)
480 idx = 255;
481 if (idx < 0)
482 idx = 0;
483 rgb.r = rgb.g = rgb.b = 0;
484 if (idx < 16) {
485 rgb.r = StdColors[idx].r;
486 rgb.g = StdColors[idx].g;
487 rgb.b = StdColors[idx].b;
488 } else if (idx >= 16 && idx <= 231) {
489 idx -= 16;
490 rgb.r = (idx / 36) % 6 * 51;
491 rgb.g = (idx / 6) % 6 * 51;
492 rgb.b = (idx % 6) * 51;
493 } else if (idx >= 232 && idx <= 255) {
494 int gray = (idx - 232) * 11;
495 rgb.r = rgb.g = rgb.b = gray;
496 }
497 return rgb;
498}
StdColors
RGB StdColors[16]

◆ xwgetch()

int xwgetch ( WINDOW * win,
Chyron * chyron )
extern

Wrapper for wgetch that handles signals and mouse events.

Parameters
winPointer to window
chyronPointer to chyron for handling chyron line clicks
Returns
Key code or ERR if interrupted by signal
Note
This, of course, will be expanded into an event loop for message queuing

Get mouse event and check if it's a left click or double click. If the click is outside the window, ignore it. If it's on the chyron line, get the corresponding key command. Otherwise, store the click coordinates as click_y and click_x for later use.

Definition at line 1075 of file dwin.c.

1075 {
1076 int c;
1077 MEVENT event;
1078 event.y = event.x = -1;
1079 click_y = click_x = -1;
1080 halfdelay(1);
1081 do {
1082 c = wgetch(win);
1083 if (sig_received != 0) {
1084 if (handle_signal(sig_received))
1085 c = display_error(em0, em1, em2, nullptr);
1086 if (c == 'q' || c == KEY_F(9))
1087 exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
1088 continue;
1089 }
1090 if (c == ERR)
1091 continue;
1092 if (c == KEY_MOUSE) {
1093 if (getmouse(&event) != OK) {
1094 c = 0;
1095 continue;
1096 }
1097 if (event.bstate & BUTTON1_CLICKED ||
1098 event.bstate & BUTTON1_DOUBLE_CLICKED) {
1099 if (!wenclose(win, event.y, event.x)) {
1100 c = 0;
1101 continue;
1102 }
1103 wmouse_trafo(win, &event.y, &event.x, false);
1104 if (event.y < 0 || event.x < 0 || event.x >= getmaxx(win) ||
1105 event.y >= getmaxy(win)) {
1106 c = 0;
1107 continue;
1108 }
1109 click_y = event.y;
1110 click_x = event.x;
1111 if (chyron && event.y == getmaxy(win) - 1)
1112 return get_chyron_key(chyron, event.x);
1113 else
1114 return KEY_MOUSE;
1115 }
1116 }
1117 } while (c == ERR);
1118 return c;
1119}

◆ bw_ho

const wchar_t bw_ho
extern

The following are the actual wchar_t variables that will hold the box drawing characters. These correspond to the above Unicode code points. By defining them as wchar_t, we can use them in the ncurses library to draw boxes and lines in the terminal. The variables are named with a "bw_" prefix to indicate that they are box (wide) drawing characters, and they will be initialized with the corresponding Unicode characters defined above. horizontal line

horizontal line
